data.title: Regentis Biomaterials Prices IPO Of 1.25 Mln Shares At $8.00/shr
data.description:
JERUSALEM (dpa-AFX) - Regentis Biomaterials Ltd. (RGNT) announced that it has priced its initial public offering of 1.25 million ordinary shares at $8.00 per share, generating expected gross proceeds of approximately $10 million before underwriting discounts and offering expenses.

The company has also granted underwriters a 45-day option to purchase up to an additional 187,500 ordinary shares to cover potential over-allotments.

The ordinary shares are scheduled to begin trading on the NYSE American LLC on December 4, 2025, under the ticker symbol RGNT. The offering is anticipated to close on or about December 5, 2025, subject to the satisfaction of customary closing conditions.

The company intends to use the net proceeds from the Offering primarily for development activities, including the completion of its pivotal trial, and

preparation of the PMA submission for GerlinC, and also for corporate purposes

including the repayment of certain debt and deferred compensation payments.

data.title: Australian Stocks Snap Two-Day Gain
data.description:
The S&P/ASX 200 fell 0.3% to around 8,570 on Thursday, halting its two-session winning streak, as losses in gold stocks and rate-sensitive sectors offset strength in miners.

Gold miners Newmont and Northern Star dropped around 1.9% each, pulling the broader sector 1.3% lower.

Financials and real estate stocks also retreated 0.4% and 2.2%, respectively.

Three of the “Big Four” banks traded in the red, while property giant Goodman Group slipped 1.5%.

Wednesday’s Q3 GDP data and elevated inflation strengthened the view that the economy may have hit its growth limit, shifting bets toward a potential rate hike.

Household spending beating forecasts in October, its biggest rise since January 2024, further supported the case.

Meanwhile, heavyweight miners rose more than 1% to a record high, as copper prices surged to an all-time peak, bolstered by a weaker dollar and supply concerns.

Sector giant BHP surged nearly 3% to a one-month high, while Rio Tinto soared 2.4% to an all-time high.

data.title: FTSE Bursa Malaysia KLCI Index Opens 0.1% Higher at 1624.63
Key facts: Rio Tinto shares rise on record copper prices; J.P. Morgan bullish
data.description:
• Rio Tinto (RIO) shares rose 1% to 3.5% as copper prices hit a record $11,502 per metric ton, fueled by tight global supply and strong U.S. shipments.12
  • J.P. Morgan has identified Rio Tinto as a conviction large-cap play in the EMEA mining sector, emphasizing the potential for significant value to be unlocked in 2026 under its new CEO.3
  • Rio Tinto is set to announce cost cuts and operational streamlining under CEO Simon Trott, potentially narrowing the free cash flow gap with BHP. Macquarie raised its 12-month price target to A$130.4

data.title: Key facts: Barclays struggles in stress test; faces mis-selling scandal; share buyback completed
data.description:
• Barclays faced challenges in the Bank of England's 2025 stress test, mainly due to its riskier international and consumer lending, impacting its common equity Tier 1 (CET1) ratios.1
  • Barclays faces a mis-selling scandal over motor finance deals from 2007, as the UK's Financial Conduct Authority will resume handling related complaints on May 31, 2026.2
  • On December 3, 2025, Barclays PLC repurchased 9,744,173 shares at an average price of 431.03p each, reducing its issued share capital to 13,901,949,602 shares.3

data.title: Key facts: Unilever's Magnum Ice Cream to Lead Market; Audit Finds Issues at Ben & Jerry's Foundation
data.description:
• Unilever's Magnum Ice Cream will be the largest standalone ice cream brand, holding over 20% of the $87 billion market, aiming to boost efficiency and manage raw material costs.1
  • On December 3, 2025, Unilever remained in the FTSE UK Index Series after the demerger of The Magnum Ice Cream Company, now a non-constituent.2
  • An audit of the Ben & Jerry's Foundation, associated with Unilever, revealed deficiencies in financial controls and governance.3

data.title: Key facts: HSBC's chairman targets fee income; Fitch affirms 'A+' rating
data.description:
• HSBC's chairman, Nelson, will lead efforts to increase fee income amid falling interest income due to rate cuts, while shifting the bank's focus towards Asia after a major restructuring.1
  • On December 3, 2025, Fitch Ratings noted HSBC's strong capital levels in the Bank of England's stress test, with minimal impact on capital ratios, supporting its 'A+' rating.2

data.title: Key facts: Glencore's stock hits 10-month high; 1,000 roles eliminated; layoffs in chrome venture
data.description:
• Glencore's stock rose 6.3% to a 10-month high after announcing a copper production target of 1.6 million tons by 2035, despite lowering 2026 guidance to 840,000 tons.1
  • Glencore has eliminated 1,000 roles as part of a new operating structure, with cost-saving initiatives expected to be fully implemented by the end of 2026.2
  • Glencore's chrome venture with Merafe will lay off workers as electricity tariffs threaten two ferrochrome smelters in South Africa, set to close on January 1, 2025, without a government plan.3

data.title: Key facts: GSK Buyback Reaches 237M Shares; New Pricing Regime Planned
data.description:
• On December 3, 2025, GSK plc acquired 11,546,733 shares in its buyback program, raising total treasury shares to 237,011,577, or 5.81% of voting rights.1
  • GSK executives are set to collaborate with the British government in developing a new pricing regime to replace the criticized VPAG scheme.2

data.title: Key facts: BP Buys Back Shares; Gains 1.3% Amid Rising Oil Prices
data.description:
• On December 3, 2025, BP p.l.c. bought back 1,495,824 ordinary shares as part of a buyback program initiated on November 4, 2025, approved by shareholders.1
  • BP gained 1.3% as energy stocks advanced 0.7%, tracking a rise in oil prices.2
  • BP and other oil companies opposed Colonial Pipeline's proposed changes to gasoline shipments, arguing that the changes would harm their businesses by shifting blending margins to Colonial.3

data.title: Details of failed studies cast pall on Novo Nordisk's GLP-1 as Alzheimer's treatment
data.description:
Key points:
  • Trials see no cognitive benefit from semaglutide for Alzheimer's
  • Modest improvement seen in about a quarter of measured biomarkers
  • Safety and tolerability consistent with previous trials

By Deena Beasley and Julie Steenhuysen

Details from two large trials of Novo Nordisk's GLP-1 drug semaglutide showed it provided no cognitive benefit for people with early Alzheimer's disease, researchers said at a medical meeting on Wednesday, dashing remaining hopes that the widely used medication could help such patients.

The Danish drugmaker said last week that the trials, which were designed to show that its semaglutide pill Rybelsus could slow progression of the brain-wasting disease by at least 20%, did not meet their goals.

The joint studies showed some improvement in a few key biological measures of the disease, but the changes were small and not enough to make a difference in delaying cognitive decline.

Both trials showed that Rybelsus had no impact at all on the rate of decline in a clinical dementia score compared to a placebo after two years, according to results presented at the Clinical Trials in Alzheimer's Disease meeting in San Diego.

"I don't see that it affects anything that is likely to affect Alzheimer's disease," said Dr. Mary Sano, a Mount Sinai Alzheimer's researcher and a key investigator on the studies.

Alzheimer's, which gradually destroys memory and thinking skills, is characterized by changes in the brain including buildup of amyloid beta plaques and tau tangles that result in loss of neurons responsible for transmitting information.

Novo's trials, which involved 3,800 participants with confirmed Alzheimer's, did show that Rybelsus led to reductions of up to 10% in some Alzheimer's biomarkers including several measures of tau, but most of the benefits observed were below 10%.

"We know that amyloid removal needs to be much more rigorous before we see an effect," Sano said.

The two drugs currently approved for slowing Alzheimer's, Eli Lilly's Kisunla and Leqembi from Eisai and Biogen , work by removing those amyloid deposits and were shown in trials to delay disease progression by around 30%.

Novo said the safety profile of Rybelsus was consistent with the daily pill's approved use as a diabetes treatment. Semaglutide, also sold as a weekly injection as Ozempic for diabetes and Wegovy for weight loss, is associated with side effects such as nausea.

POPULATION STUDY LIMITATIONS

Much of the evidence suggesting a cognitive benefit from GLP-1s came from large population studies of people with diabetes. Novo suggested, however, that those studies had "biases," and may have overstated the drug's impact.

Peter Johannsen, Novo's international medical vice president, on Tuesday said diabetes patients prescribed GLP-1s likely had access to endocrinologists, rather than only primary care, and may be in higher socioeconomic groups than the general population.

A Novo spokesperson said the company plans to discontinue both trials and is reviewing all data from the studies, adding that "it is too early to speculate" about whether the company will plan further research in Alzheimer's disease.

Full results of the Rybelsus trials will be presented at the 2026 Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s Diseases Conferences in March.
